_work.title: Greco-Egyptian & Greco-Roman Ritual Magic
_work.creator: various
_work.place: Roman Egypt and the Mediterranean world
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The material remains of ritual magic in the Greco-Roman and Roman-Egyptian world: spell books on papyrus (the PGM), lead curse tablets, pierced binding figurines, inscribed amulets and engraved magical gems bearing deities like Abrasax and Chnoubis.

entries.title: Leaf from the Greek Magical Papyri (PGM)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
entries.summary:
A leaf from the corpus modern scholars call the *Papyri Graecae Magicae* (PGM), the working handbooks of late-antique ritual specialists.

## Iconography
Dense columns of Greek cursive set out a practical spell: an invocation, a list of [[motif:voces magicae|voces magicae]] (untranslatable power-words), instructions for offerings, and the desired result. Many such leaves intersperse charaktêres and divine names with the running text.

## Significance
The PGM are the single richest source for how magic was actually performed in the Greco-Roman world. Assembled chiefly from a Theban cache and published by Karl Preisendanz, they blend Egyptian, Greek, Jewish and Christian divine names into a syncretic ritual technology. Hans Dieter Betz's English translation made them central to the study of ancient religion and [[motif:syncretism|syncretism]].

## For artists & game designers
The layout — running prose broken by clusters of nonsense power-words and drawn signs — is a ready template for an in-world spellbook page.

citations.text: Hans Dieter Betz (ed.), *The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation* (1986).

entries.title: Magical Papyrus: Love Charm (BNUS inv. 1167)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
entries.summary:
A papyrus love charm (*charme d'amour*), one genre of the erotic-binding spells that fill the Greek magical handbooks.

## Iconography
The sheet carries a Greek spell text designed to inflame and compel a named target's desire. Such *agōgē* ("leading") charms typically name the victim and her mother, invoke chthonic and astral powers, and demand the target feel no rest until she comes to the operator.

## Significance
Erotic spells are among the best-attested categories of ancient magic, surviving on papyri, lead tablets and ostraca. They reveal the coercive, often violent emotional logic of [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]] (*katadesis*) and have been central to studies of gender and agency in antiquity by scholars such as Christopher Faraone (*Ancient Greek Love Magic*).

## For artists & game designers
A love charm makes a morally ambiguous quest item: powerful, effective, and unsettling in what it asks the caster to wish on another person.

citations.text: Christopher A. Faraone, *Ancient Greek Love Magic* (1999).

entries.title: Leiden Magical Papyrus I 384 (PGM XII)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
entries.summary:
Leiden Papyrus I 384, edited as PGM XII, a bilingual Greek-and-Demotic magical handbook from the Theban cache now in the Rijksmuseum van Oudheden.

## Iconography
The papyrus carries spell texts in both Greek and Demotic Egyptian script. Recipes cover invocations, amulet manufacture and ritual procedure, mixing Egyptian priestly knowledge with Greek formulae.

## Significance
The Leiden and London Demotic magical papyri are key evidence that the PGM grew out of Egyptian temple scriptoria. The bilingual format shows native Egyptian priests adapting their craft for a Hellenized clientele, a process documented by Jacco Dieleman (*Priests, Tongues, and Rites*). The roll belongs to the same find as Leiden I 383 and the Great Magical Papyrus of Paris.

## For artists & game designers
A bilingual scroll signals a manuscript at the seam of two cultures — useful for a relic that only a doubly-literate scribe can fully read.

entries.title: Lead Curse Tablet (British Museum 1934,11-5.1)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Greco-Roman)
entries.summary:
An inscribed lead *defixio* (curse tablet) in the British Museum, one of the most characteristic artifacts of ancient binding magic.

## Iconography
A thin sheet of lead is incised with a curse text, the soft metal taking a sharp stylus easily. Such tablets were typically rolled or folded, sometimes pierced with a nail, and deposited in graves, wells or sanctuaries to carry the message to the underworld.

## Significance
Defixiones (Greek *katadesmoi*) sought to "bind" rivals in love, litigation, commerce or the circus by consigning their names to chthonic powers. Thousands survive across the Roman world. John Gager's *Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World* and Henk Versnel's work established them as a primary window into everyday anxieties and the social reach of [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]].

## For artists & game designers
Lead's dull grey and the act of nailing-and-burying make a vivid, tactile mechanic for a curse the player must physically hide.

citations.text: John G. Gager, *Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World* (1992).

entries.title: Defixio Naming Rhodine and Faustus (Terme)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman)
entries.summary:
A Roman lead curse tablet, held in the Museo Nazionale Romano (Terme di Diocleziano), that names Rhodine and Faustus.

## Iconography
The lead sheet bears a scratched Latin curse text invoking the dead or underworld powers against the named individuals. The hand is irregular, scored quickly into the metal.

## Significance
This tablet exemplifies the personal, often erotic or competitive grievances behind Roman *defixiones*: a named target is handed over to chthonic forces. The naming of specific persons makes such tablets, like the famous Rhodine curse, both legal-historical and emotional documents of ordinary Roman life, studied within Gager's and Audollent's corpora of *defixionum tabellae*.

## For artists & game designers
Use a real named target ("I bind Rhodine…") to make an in-game curse feel intimate and specific rather than generic.

entries.title: Curse Tablets from Aquae Sulis, Bath
entries.creator: Anonymous (Romano-British)
entries.summary:
A display of lead curse tablets recovered from the sacred spring at Aquae Sulis (Bath), addressed to the goddess Sulis Minerva.

## Iconography
Dozens of small inscribed lead sheets, many concerning stolen property — cloaks, coins, gloves — beg the goddess to harm the thief until restitution is made. Several reverse the letters or list suspects.

## Significance
The Bath tablets, edited by Roger Tomlin, are the largest single group of Latin curse texts and the basis for the category Henk Versnel named "prayers for justice" — appeals to a deity for redress rather than purely aggressive [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]]. They are invaluable evidence for vulgar Latin and everyday religion in Roman Britain.

## For artists & game designers
The theft-curse — "whoever stole my cloak, let him pay" — is a perfect low-stakes magical quest hook grounded in real grievance.

entries.title: Bound Female Magical Figurine (Louvre E 27145)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
entries.rights: CC BY 2.5 (Marie-Lan Nguyen). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The Louvre's famous bound and pierced female figurine (E 27145), often loosely called a "voodoo doll," made for an erotic binding spell.

## Iconography
A kneeling nude woman of unbaked clay, arms bound behind her back, her body transfixed by thirteen needles driven into head, eyes, ears, mouth, breasts, hands, feet and genitals. She was found inside a clay vessel with a lead tablet inscribed with the spell.

## Significance
The figurine enacts PGM IV's instructions almost verbatim: pierce each part of the target so she can think of nothing but the operator. It is the most-cited object for the brutal mechanics of ancient [[motif:love magic|love magic]] and *katadesis*, discussed by Faraone, Gager and Daniel Ogden as the physical counterpart of the agōgē papyrus charms.

## For artists & game designers
A pierced figurine in a sealed jar is an instantly legible — and disquieting — magical object; the needle-per-body-part logic doubles as a ritual mini-game.

citations.text: Christopher A. Faraone, *Ancient Greek Love Magic* (1999).

entries.title: Inscribed Gold Sheet (Lamella) Amulet
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (The British Museum / PAS). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A thin inscribed gold sheet (*lamella*) of the kind worn as a protective amulet or phylactery, recorded through the Portable Antiquities Scheme.

## Iconography
A small rectangle of beaten gold foil incised with text — typically divine names, [[motif:voces magicae|voces magicae]] and protective formulae. Such sheets were rolled or folded tightly and carried in a tubular case (*bulla* or capsule) against the body.

## Significance
Gold and silver lamellae are the high-end of ancient protective magic, used against fever, the evil eye and demons. Roy Kotansky's *Greek Magical Amulets* catalogues the genre, which links the Greco-Egyptian papyri to later Jewish and Christian phylactery traditions. The precious metal signals both wealth and the seriousness of the threat warded against.

## For artists & game designers
A rolled gold strip in a tiny capsule is a wearable charm with built-in mystery: the text is hidden until unrolled, risking its power.

entries.title: Magical Gem Depicting Abrasax
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
entries.summary:
An engraved magical gem (intaglio) bearing the anguipede deity Abrasax (Abraxas), the signature figure of Greco-Egyptian gem magic.

## Iconography
The composite god has a rooster's head, a human torso in armour, and two serpents for legs, usually holding a whip and a round shield. The shield or reverse often carries the name IAΩ or other [[motif:voces magicae|voces magicae]].

## Significance
The Greek letters of ABRASAX sum to 365, the days of the year, and the figure became associated with the Gnostic teacher Basilides and the cosmic ruler of the 365 heavens. As Campbell Bonner showed in *Studies in Magical Amulets*, such gems were worn for protection and were among the most reproduced magical images of antiquity, later mined by Renaissance and modern occultism.

## For artists & game designers
The rooster-snake-soldier hybrid is a ready-made arcane sigil — recognisable, weird, and rich with numerological lore (365).

entries.title: Intaglio Gem of Chnoubis (BnF, de Clercq 3453)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman Egypt)
entries.summary:
An engraved gem in the Bibliothèque nationale de France (de Clercq collection) showing Chnoubis, the lion-headed serpent of ancient healing magic.

## Iconography
A serpent with a radiate lion's head rears up, usually over a horizontal bar; the reverse frequently bears the triple-S-and-bar Chnoubis sign and his name. The figure draws on Egyptian solar and Agathodaimon imagery.

## Significance
Chnoubis gems were specifically therapeutic, worn against ailments of the stomach and digestion — Galen even mentions green jasper amulets for the stomach. As studied by Bonner, Simone Michel and Véronique Dasen, they show ancient magic operating as practical medicine, targeting the body part depicted. The recurring "Chnoubis sign" is one of the few standardised [[motif:charakteres|charaktêres]] of antiquity.

## For artists & game designers
A lion-serpent carved into green stone makes a believable healing talisman keyed to a specific affliction (the stomach).

entries.title: Chrysopoeia of Cleopatra (Ouroboros)
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Cleopatra the Alchemist)
entries.date: Surviving copy medieval; tradition Greco-Roman (1st–3rd c. CE)
entries.summary:
The *Chrysopoeia of Cleopatra*, an emblem from the Greco-Egyptian alchemical tradition centred on a serpent devouring its own tail.

## Iconography
A double ring encloses the [[motif:ouroboros|ouroboros]] — a serpent biting its tail, half light and half dark — around the motto *hen to pan*, "the all is one." Adjacent diagrams give the symbols and apparatus of gold-making (chrysopoeia).

## Significance
Attributed to Cleopatra the Alchemist, this is among the earliest and most influential statements of the ouroboros as a symbol of cyclical unity and material transmutation. Transmitted through the Greek alchemical corpus (Zosimos, the Marciana and Paris manuscripts), it became foundational for later alchemy and for Jung's reading of the ouroboros as a symbol of self-renewal.

## For artists & game designers
The light/dark ouroboros and "all is one" motto are a compact, ready-to-engrave emblem for an alchemical faction or transmutation system.

citations.text: Marcellin Berthelot, *Collection des anciens alchimistes grecs* (1888).

entries.title: Sator Square, Palaestra at Pompeii
entries.creator: Anonymous (Roman)
entries.summary:
The Sator (Rotas) square scratched in the Palaestra at Pompeii — the earliest securely dated example of this famous magical word-square.

## Iconography
Five five-letter words, SATOR AREPO TENET OPERA ROTAS, form a grid readable identically across, down, and in both reversed directions: a perfect bidirectional palindrome built around the cross-shaped TENET.

## Significance
Buried by Vesuvius in 79 CE, the Pompeii example proves the square predates Christianity's spread in the West. Its letters can be rearranged into PATERNOSTER twice (with two each of A and O, read as alpha and omega), fuelling debate over Christian, Jewish or purely apotropaic origins. It recurs for over a millennium on amulets, walls and in folk medicine across Europe as a protective and [[motif:apotropaic|apotropaic]] charm.

## For artists & game designers
A self-mirroring word grid is an elegant puzzle-glyph: it reads the same from every direction, perfect for a ward that must be drawn exactly.

entries.title: Amuletic Pendant with Holy Rider (Walters 54.2653)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine / Eastern Mediterranean)
entries.date: Late antique – early Byzantine (5th–7th century CE)
entries.summary:
A late-antique amuletic pendant in the Walters Art Museum showing the "Holy Rider" — a horseman spearing a prostrate demon, an image tied to King Solomon's mastery over evil spirits.

## Iconography
A mounted figure drives a spear down into a fallen female demon beneath the horse's hooves. The reverse often bears the formula "One God who conquers evil" and the device against the *baskania* (evil eye).

## Significance
The Holy Rider amulet bridges Greco-Egyptian magic and Christian late antiquity. The rider is variously named Solomon, an angel, or a saint, trampling the child-killing demon Gyllou/Abyzou. Such amulets, studied by Bonner and Jeffrey Spier, show how the legend of Solomon as exorcist (the *Testament of Solomon*) fused with older protective gem traditions into a Christianised [[motif:binding magic|binding magic]].

## For artists & game designers
The rider-tramples-demon composition is a clean, heraldic emblem for a protective order or anti-demon faction.

citations.text: Jeffrey Spier, "Medieval Byzantine Magical Amulets," *Journal of the Warburg and Courtauld Institutes* (1993).

entries.title: Bronze Amulet of the God Bes (Museo Egizio C 672)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Egypt)
entries.summary:
A bronze figure of Bes from the Museo Egizio, Turin — the dwarf-god whose grotesque face guarded households, mothers and children, and who underlies the composite Bes-Pantheos magical figures.

## Iconography
A squat, frontal dwarf deity with a lion-like bearded face, plumed crown and protruding tongue. In magical contexts Bes proliferates into the "Bes-Pantheos": a many-armed, multi-winged, animal-headed composite gathering many gods' powers into one apotropaic body.

## Significance
Bes was protective rather than infernal — warding off snakes, demons and the dangers of childbirth — and his image survived from pharaonic Egypt into the Greco-Roman magical repertoire. The Bes-Pantheos figures on gems and papyri concentrate cosmic power for protection and were catalogued among Greco-Egyptian magical types by Bonner and the *Campbell Bonner Magical Gems Database*.

## For artists & game designers
Bes's grotesque-but-friendly face is a model for an apotropaic guardian whose ugliness is the point — the scarier the ward, the safer the home.

entries.title: Bronze Statuette of Harpocrates (Museo Egizio C 430)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Greco-Roman Egypt)
entries.summary:
A bronze statuette of Harpocrates — Horus the Child — in the Museo Egizio, Turin, a deity central to Greco-Egyptian protective magic.

## Iconography
A nude boy with a side-lock of youth raises one finger to his lips, the gesture the Greeks read as a sign of silence and secrecy. He is frequently shown seated on a lotus, sometimes on the cippus of Horus that wards off venomous creatures.

## Significance
As the infant solar god triumphant over chaos and serpents, Harpocrates was invoked on amulets and the Horus-cippi for protection against snakebite and the dangers of childhood. The Greeks reinterpreted the childhood finger-gesture as enjoining silence, making him a patron of [[motif:secrecy|mystery and secrecy]] in the magical papyri, where he appears among the sun-god's daily forms.

## For artists & game designers
The finger-to-lips child-god is a perfect emblem for a cult of silence or a secret-keeping NPC: the iconography itself says "tell no one."

citations.text: Hans Dieter Betz (ed.), *The Greek Magical Papyri in Translation* (1986).
